expanded_description: Practical laboratory techniques for preparing solutions of known concentration, including equipment selection, step-by-step procedures, safety protocols, and quality control measures.

core_concept.principle: Accurate solution preparation requires proper equipment selection, precise measurement techniques, and systematic procedures to ensure the final concentration matches the intended value.
core_concept.explanation: Preparing solutions in the laboratory is a fundamental skill that bridges theoretical calculations with practical application. Whether dissolving a solid solute to make a standard solution or diluting a concentrated stock, following proper technique ensures accuracy, reproducibility, and safety. Key principles include using appropriate glassware for the required precision, measuring solute mass accurately, and diluting to the final volume mark—not adding a calculated volume of solvent.

image.alt: Diagram showing laboratory equipment for solution preparation: balance, volumetric flask, pipet, and graduated cylinder
image.caption: Proper equipment and technique ensure accurate solution preparation

sections.section_title: Essential Laboratory Equipment for Solution Preparation
sections.introduction: Selecting the right equipment is the first step in preparing accurate solutions. Different tasks require different levels of precision, and choosing appropriate glassware and instruments ensures reliable results.

key_concept.definition: Solution preparation equipment is classified by precision: analytical balances and volumetric glassware for high-precision work; top-loading balances and graduated cylinders for approximate work.
key_concept.context: The choice of equipment depends on the required accuracy of the final concentration and the nature of the solute (solid, liquid, concentrated, hazardous).

principles.statement: Select equipment whose precision matches the required accuracy of the final solution concentration.
principles.application: For standard solutions ($\pm 0.1\%$ accuracy), use analytical balance ($\pm 0.0001$ g) and Class A volumetric glassware; for approximate solutions ($\pm 5\%$), use top-loading balance and graduated cylinders.
principles.example: Preparing $0.1000$ M NaCl standard: analytical balance for mass, volumetric flask for final volume; preparing $0.1$ M rinse solution: top-loading balance, graduated cylinder acceptable.
principles.analogy: Like choosing a ruler: use a micrometer for engine parts, a tape measure for room dimensions.

principles.statement: Volumetric glassware is calibrated to contain (TC) or to deliver (TD) a specific volume at a specified temperature.
principles.application: Volumetric flasks are TC (contain); pipets and burets are TD (deliver); always use at calibrated temperature ($20^\circ\text{C}$) for maximum accuracy.
principles.example: A $100.0$ mL volumetric flask contains exactly $100.0$ mL when filled to the mark at $20^\circ\text{C}$; a $10.00$ mL pipet delivers exactly $10.00$ mL when properly drained.
principles.analogy: Like a measuring cup: 'fill to this line' (TC) vs. 'pour out this amount' (TD).

components.definition: Key equipment for solution preparation includes balances for mass measurement and volumetric glassware for volume measurement.
components.interpretation: Relative error decreases with larger measured values; weigh more solute or prepare larger volumes when possible to improve accuracy.

image.alt: Labeled diagram of analytical balance, volumetric flask, pipet, and graduated cylinder
image.caption: Essential equipment: balance for mass, volumetric flask for final volume, pipet for precise transfers

worked_examples.title: Selecting Equipment for Different Precision Requirements
worked_examples.problem: You need to prepare (a) $1.000$ L of $0.1000$ M $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ standard solution and (b) $500$ mL of approximately $0.1$ M NaCl for rinsing. What equipment should you use for each?

step_by_step.explanation: Standard solution requires high precision ($\pm 0.1\%$); concentration specified to 4 significant figures
Analytical balance ($\pm 0.0001$ g) for mass; Class A $1.000$ L volumetric flask for final volume; volumetric pipet if diluting
step_by_step.calculation: Equipment: analytical balance, $1.000$ L volumetric flask, funnel, stirring rod

step_by_step.explanation: Rinse solution requires approximate concentration ($\pm 5\%$ acceptable); concentration specified to 1 significant figure
Top-loading balance ($\pm 0.01$ g) sufficient; $500$ mL graduated cylinder or beaker for approximate volume
step_by_step.calculation: Equipment: top-loading balance, $500$ mL graduated cylinder, stirring rod

worked_examples.final_answer: (a) Analytical balance + $1.000$ L volumetric flask; (b) Top-loading balance + graduated cylinder
worked_examples.concept_applied: Match equipment precision to required solution accuracy.

worked_examples.title: Understanding TC vs. TD Glassware
worked_examples.problem: Explain why you should not use a $100$ mL graduated cylinder to prepare $100$ mL of $0.100$ M solution, even though it is marked '100 mL'.

step_by_step.explanation: Graduated cylinders are approximate measuring devices, not precision volumetric glassware
Class A $100$ mL volumetric flask has tolerance $\pm 0.08$ mL ($\pm 0.08\%$); graduated cylinder may have $\pm 1$ mL ($\pm 1\%$) or worse
If final volume is off by $1\%$, concentration is off by $1\%$; for a $0.100$ M solution, this means $0.099$–$0.101$ M vs. $0.0999$–$0.1001$ M
Use volumetric flasks for preparing solutions of known concentration; use graduated cylinders only for approximate measurements

worked_examples.final_answer: Graduated cylinders lack the precision of volumetric flasks; use volumetric flask for accurate solution preparation
worked_examples.concept_applied: Volumetric flasks are calibrated for precise final volume; graduated cylinders are for approximate measurements.

sections.key_concept: Preparing a solution from a solid solute involves accurately weighing the solute, dissolving it in solvent, and diluting to the final volume in a volumetric flask.

classifications.value: Systematic approach ensures accurate, reproducible solution preparation
classifications.behavior: Follow each step in order; do not skip or rearrange steps

examples.explanation: Calculate mass ($0.100 \text{ mol/L} \times 0.500 \text{ L} \times 58.44 \text{ g/mol} = 2.922$ g); weigh on analytical balance; dissolve in ~$300$ mL water in beaker; transfer to $500$ mL volumetric flask; rinse beaker into flask; dilute to mark; mix by inverting 10×

classifications.value: Hydrated compounds require molar mass adjustment to account for water of hydration
characteristics: Water adds mass but not reactive component
Must use hydrated molar mass for weighing
classifications.behavior: Calculate mass using hydrated molar mass; the water of hydration becomes part of the solvent

examples.explanation: Hydrated MM = $249.70$ g/mol; anhydrous MM = $159.62$ g/mol; to prepare $0.100$ M $\text{Cu}^{2+}$, weigh $24.97$ g hydrated or $15.96$ g anhydrous per liter

classifications.value: Some solids absorb water or decompose; special handling required
classifications.behavior: Minimize exposure to air; verify concentration by titration if solute is unstable

examples.explanation: Absorb $\text{CO}_2$ and water from air; weigh quickly, prepare solution, standardize against primary standard (e.g., KHP)
Loses water of hydration; prepare solution, let stand 1–2 days, then standardize

worked_examples.title: Preparing a Standard Solution from Solid
worked_examples.problem: Describe how to prepare $250.0$ mL of $0.0500$ M $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$ from solid $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$. (Molar mass = $294.18$ g/mol)

step_by_step.explanation: Use $n = M \times V$; then mass = $n \times MM$
step_by_step.calculation: $n = 0.0500 \text{ mol/L} \times 0.2500 \text{ L} = 0.01250 \text{ mol}$; mass = $0.01250 \text{ mol} \times 294.18 \text{ g/mol} = 3.677$ g

step_by_step.explanation: Use analytical balance; weigh by difference for highest accuracy
step_by_step.calculation: Tare weighing boat; add $\sim 3.7$ g; record exact mass (e.g., $3.6772$ g)

step_by_step.explanation: Transfer to clean beaker; add $\sim 100$ mL distilled water; stir until completely dissolved
step_by_step.calculation: Ensure no solid remains; gentle heating if needed (cool before transferring)

step_by_step.explanation: Quantitatively transfer solution to $250.0$ mL volumetric flask using funnel; rinse beaker and stirring rod 3× with small portions of water, adding rinsings to flask
step_by_step.calculation: Ensures all solute reaches final volume

step_by_step.explanation: Add distilled water until bottom of meniscus is level with calibration mark at eye level
Stopper flask; invert 10–15 times to ensure homogeneity
step_by_step.calculation: Solution is now $0.0500$ M (based on exact mass weighed)

worked_examples.final_answer: Weigh $3.677$ g $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$, dissolve, transfer to $250.0$ mL volumetric flask, dilute to mark, mix
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic procedure ensures accurate standard solution preparation.

worked_examples.title: Handling Hydrated Solutes
worked_examples.problem: How much $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ is needed to prepare $500.0$ mL of $0.100$ M $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ solution? (Molar mass hydrated = $249.70$ g/mol)

step_by_step.explanation: Each formula unit of $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$ provides one $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ion
Use hydrated molar mass; water of hydration becomes part of solvent
Weigh $12.485$ g hydrated $\text{CuSO}_4$, dissolve, transfer to $500.0$ mL volumetric flask, dilute to mark, mix

worked_examples.final_answer: Weigh $12.49$ g $\text{CuSO}_4 \cdot 5\text{H}_2\text{O}$; prepare as standard solution
worked_examples.concept_applied: Use hydrated molar mass for weighing; water of hydration does not affect final concentration calculation.

sections.introduction: Dilution is the most common method for preparing working solutions from concentrated stock solutions. Proper technique ensures the final concentration is accurate and the procedure is safe.

key_concept.definition: Dilution involves measuring a precise volume of concentrated stock solution and adding solvent to reach a larger final volume, reducing concentration while keeping moles of solute constant.
key_concept.context: The dilution formula $C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$ guides calculations, but laboratory technique determines whether the calculated concentration is achieved in practice.

principles.statement: When diluting concentrated acids or bases, always add the concentrated reagent to water, never water to concentrated reagent.
principles.application: Adding water to concentrated acid can cause violent boiling and splashing due to heat of dilution; adding acid to water allows heat to dissipate safely.
principles.example: Diluting concentrated $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$: add acid slowly to water with stirring; never reverse the order.
principles.analogy: Like adding hot oil to water vs. water to hot oil—one is safe, the other causes dangerous splattering.

principles.statement: All solute must be transferred from the measuring device to the volumetric flask to achieve the calculated concentration.
principles.application: Rinse pipet or graduated cylinder with solvent and add rinsings to the volumetric flask to ensure complete transfer.
principles.example: After delivering stock solution from pipet to flask, rinse pipet tip with distilled water and add rinsings to flask.
principles.analogy: Like getting every drop of syrup from a bottle—rinse to get it all.

categories.description: Highest precision method for measuring stock solution volume
categories.detailed_mechanism: Volumetric pipets are calibrated to deliver (TD) a specific volume; use pipet bulb for safe aspiration; drain by gravity with tip touching flask wall

examples.explanation: Use $10.00$ mL volumetric pipet to transfer stock to $1.000$ L volumetric flask; dilute to mark

categories.type: Dilution with Graduated Pipet or Cylinder
categories.description: Approximate method for less critical applications
categories.detailed_mechanism: Graduated devices have lower precision; read meniscus at eye level; estimate between marks if needed

examples.explanation: Use graduated cylinder to measure stock; acceptable for $\pm 5\%$ accuracy

categories.description: Preparing a series of progressively diluted solutions from one stock
categories.detailed_mechanism: Each dilution uses the previous solution as stock; use clean/dry glassware for each step to avoid contamination

examples.explanation: Prepare $100$, $50$, $25$, $12.5$ ppm standards by serial $1:2$ dilutions from $100$ ppm stock

worked_examples.title: Safe Dilution of Concentrated Acid
worked_examples.problem: Describe the safe procedure for preparing $500.0$ mL of $1.00$ M $\text{HCl}$ from concentrated ($12.0$ M) $\text{HCl}$.

step_by_step.explanation: Wear goggles, gloves, lab coat; work in fume hood; have water and spill kit nearby

step_by_step.step: Add water to volumetric flask first
step_by_step.explanation: Place ~$300$ mL distilled water in $500.0$ mL volumetric flask

step_by_step.step: Measure and add concentrated acid carefully
step_by_step.explanation: Use graduated cylinder or pipet to measure $41.7$ mL conc. $\text{HCl}$; slowly add to flask with gentle swirling; solution will warm
step_by_step.calculation: Add acid to water, never water to acid

step_by_step.explanation: Allow solution to cool to room temperature (volume changes with temperature); add distilled water to calibration mark; mix by inverting 10×

worked_examples.final_answer: Add $41.7$ mL conc. $\text{HCl}$ slowly to ~$300$ mL water in $500$ mL flask, cool, dilute to mark, mix
worked_examples.concept_applied: Safety and technique are as important as calculation in dilution procedures.

worked_examples.title: Serial Dilution for Standard Curve
worked_examples.problem: Prepare $50.0$ mL each of $10.0$, $5.00$, $2.50$, and $1.25$ ppm $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ standards from a $100$ ppm stock using serial $1:2$ dilutions.

step_by_step.explanation: Each step: take $25.0$ mL of previous solution, dilute to $50.0$ mL total ($1:2$ dilution)
$V_1 = (10.0 \text{ ppm} \times 50.0 \text{ mL})/100 \text{ ppm} = 5.00 \text{ mL}$; pipet $5.00$ mL stock into $50.0$ mL volumetric flask; dilute to mark; mix
Pipet $25.0$ mL of $10.0$ ppm solution into clean $50.0$ mL volumetric flask; dilute to mark; mix
Repeat $1:2$ dilution process using previous standard as stock
Label each flask with concentration, date, and preparer; store in appropriate conditions

worked_examples.final_answer: Prepare $10.0$ ppm by diluting $5.00$ mL stock to $50.0$ mL; then serial $1:2$ dilutions for $5.00$, $2.50$, $1.25$ ppm
worked_examples.concept_applied: Serial dilutions efficiently prepare multiple standards with consistent technique.

sections.key_concept: Verifying the accuracy of prepared solutions ensures reliable experimental results. Quality control steps catch errors before they affect downstream work.

classifications.value: Check for undissolved solute, cloudiness, or color inconsistencies
classifications.behavior: If solution is cloudy or has particles, filter or re-prepare; do not use questionable solutions

examples.explanation: Should be clear and colorless; cloudiness indicates undissolved salt or contamination
Should be uniform purple; fading indicates decomposition; prepare fresh if needed

classifications.value: Confirm concentration by independent measurement when high accuracy is required
classifications.behavior: Standard solutions for quantitative analysis should be verified; record actual concentration for calculations

examples.explanation: Titrate against primary standard KHP; calculate exact concentration; use verified value in subsequent work
Measure absorbance of colored solution; compare to Beer's Law calibration to confirm concentration

classifications.value: Proper labeling prevents errors and ensures traceability
classifications.behavior: Label immediately after preparation; never rely on memory; update if concentration is verified/adjusted

examples.explanation: $0.1000$ M $\text{K}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7$; prepared 2024-03-15 by J. Smith; verified by titration: $0.0998$ M; expires 2024-09-15; store in dark

worked_examples.title: Standardizing a NaOH Solution
worked_examples.problem: You prepared approximately $0.1$ M NaOH by dissolving $2.0$ g NaOH in $500$ mL water. How would you determine its exact concentration?

step_by_step.explanation: Use potassium hydrogen phthalate (KHP), a stable, high-purity acid with known molar mass ($204.22$ g/mol)
Weigh three $\sim 0.5$ g samples of dried KHP into clean flasks; record exact masses
Add phenolphthalein indicator; titrate each KHP sample with NaOH solution to pale pink endpoint; record volume used
step_by_step.calculation: Example: $24.85$ mL NaOH used for $0.002509$ mol KHP

step_by_step.explanation: Moles NaOH = moles KHP (1:1); $M_{\text{NaOH}} = \text{mol NaOH}/V_{\text{NaOH}}$
Average three titrations; report mean and standard deviation; update solution label with verified concentration

worked_examples.final_answer: Standardize by titration against KHP; use verified concentration ($0.1010$ M) for calculations
worked_examples.concept_applied: Primary standard titration verifies concentration of solutions prepared from unstable solutes.

worked_examples.title: Spectrophotometric Verification
worked_examples.problem: You prepared a $0.00100$ M $\text{KMnO}_4$ solution. How can you verify its concentration using spectrophotometry?

step_by_step.explanation: $A = \varepsilon b c$, where $A$ = absorbance, $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity, $b$ = path length, $c$ = concentration
Fill clean cuvet with solution; zero spectrophotometer with blank; measure absorbance at $525$ nm
Rearrange Beer's Law: $c = A/(\varepsilon b)$
Intended: $0.00100$ M; measured: $0.000100$ M—factor of 10 difference!
step_by_step.calculation: Error likely: diluted 10× too much or misread volumetric mark

step_by_step.explanation: Check calculations and technique; re-prepare solution if verification fails
step_by_step.calculation: Always verify critical solutions before use

worked_examples.final_answer: Measure absorbance; calculate concentration via Beer's Law; compare to intended value; re-prepare if discrepancy > acceptable error
worked_examples.concept_applied: Spectrophotometry provides independent verification of colored solution concentrations.

sections.section_title: Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.key_concept: Understanding common mistakes in solution preparation helps ensure accurate, reproducible results and safe laboratory practice.

classifications.type: Error: Adding Solvent to Final Volume Instead of Diluting to Mark
classifications.value: Adding a calculated volume of solvent instead of diluting to the volumetric mark
classifications.behavior: Always dilute to the calibration mark on the volumetric flask; do not add $V_2 - V_1$ of solvent

examples.process: Wrong: add $90$ mL water to $10$ mL stock to make $100$ mL
examples.explanation: Final volume may not be exactly $100$ mL due to volume contraction/expansion

examples.process: Correct: add stock to flask, then add water to $100$ mL mark
examples.explanation: Volumetric flask ensures exact final volume

classifications.value: Failing to quantitatively transfer all solute to the volumetric flask
characteristics: Solute left in weighing boat or beaker
classifications.behavior: Rinse all containers that held solute with solvent and add rinsings to volumetric flask

examples.explanation: Rinse weighing boat, beaker, and stirring rod 3× with small portions of water; add all rinsings to flask

classifications.value: Reading volume at wrong angle or wrong part of meniscus
characteristics: Reading top instead of bottom of meniscus
classifications.behavior: Read bottom of meniscus at eye level; use white card behind flask to enhance visibility

examples.explanation: Bottom of meniscus should be tangent to mark; eye level with mark

classifications.value: Preparing or using solutions at temperatures different from calibration temperature
classifications.behavior: Prepare and use volumetric glassware at $20^\circ\text{C}$ (calibration temperature) when high accuracy is required

examples.explanation: Solution contracts on cooling; final concentration higher than calculated

classifications.value: Introducing impurities that alter concentration or reactivity
classifications.behavior: Clean glassware thoroughly; rinse with solvent before use; use dedicated equipment for each solution when possible

examples.process: Preparing acid solution in base-contaminated flask
examples.explanation: Acid neutralized by residual base; concentration lower than intended

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Incomplete Transfer
worked_examples.problem: A student prepares $0.100$ M NaCl by weighing $2.922$ g, dissolving in a beaker, and pouring into a $500$ mL volumetric flask without rinsing. The final concentration is lower than expected. Explain why and how to prevent this.

step_by_step.explanation: Solute remained in beaker and on stirring rod; not all $2.922$ g reached final volume
step_by_step.calculation: Actual moles in flask < calculated moles

step_by_step.explanation: Concentration = moles/volume; fewer moles in same volume = lower concentration
step_by_step.calculation: If $10\%$ of solute left behind, concentration is $0.090$ M instead of $0.100$ M

step_by_step.explanation: After pouring solution into flask, rinse beaker and stirring rod 3× with small portions of distilled water; add all rinsings to flask
step_by_step.calculation: Quantitative transfer ensures all solute reaches final volume

step_by_step.explanation: Always rinse containers that held solute; add rinsings to volumetric flask
step_by_step.calculation: Prevents loss of solute and concentration errors

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: incomplete transfer of solute; prevent by rinsing beaker/rod and adding rinsings to flask
worked_examples.concept_applied: Quantitative transfer ensures all solute contributes to final concentration.

worked_examples.title: Error Analysis: Meniscus Reading
worked_examples.problem: A student prepares a solution by filling a $100$ mL volumetric flask until the top of the meniscus is at the mark. How does this affect the concentration?

step_by_step.explanation: Water forms concave meniscus; bottom is lower than top
step_by_step.calculation: Bottom of meniscus is the correct reference point

step_by_step.explanation: Filling to top of meniscus means actual volume > $100$ mL
step_by_step.calculation: Volume error: $\sim +0.5$ mL for $100$ mL flask ($+0.5\%$)

step_by_step.explanation: Concentration = moles/volume; larger volume = lower concentration
Read bottom of meniscus at eye level; use white card behind flask to enhance contrast
step_by_step.calculation: Proper reading ensures accurate final volume

worked_examples.final_answer: Reading top of meniscus gives volume > mark; concentration lower than calculated; read bottom of meniscus at eye level
worked_examples.concept_applied: Proper meniscus reading ensures accurate final volume and concentration.

worked_examples.title: Solution Preparation Checklist
worked_examples.problem: Outline a systematic verification approach for solution preparation.

step_by_step.explanation: Double-check mass or volume calculations; verify molar mass and units
step_by_step.calculation: Recalculate independently or have partner verify

step_by_step.explanation: Confirm appropriate balance and glassware for required precision
step_by_step.calculation: Analytical balance + volumetric flask for standards; top-loading + cylinder for approximate

step_by_step.explanation: Weigh by difference; dissolve completely; quantitative transfer; dilute to mark; mix thoroughly
Verify solution is clear, homogeneous, and free of particles
step_by_step.calculation: If cloudy or particulate, filter or re-prepare

step_by_step.explanation: Label immediately with name, concentration, date, preparer, hazards, expiration
step_by_step.calculation: Never rely on memory; label before leaving bench

step_by_step.explanation: Standardize or verify concentration by independent method for critical solutions
step_by_step.calculation: Titration, spectrophotometry, or conductivity as appropriate

worked_examples.final_answer: Follow 6-check verification for all solution preparations
worked_examples.concept_applied: Systematic verification prevents common solution preparation errors.

sections.introduction: Test your understanding with these practical problems. Try solving before looking at solutions.

key_concept.definition: Practice problems reinforce laboratory technique concepts and calculation skills for solution preparation.
key_concept.context: Cover solutions and attempt each problem independently, then check your work.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 1
worked_examples.problem: Describe how to prepare $100.0$ mL of $0.200$ M $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ from solid $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$. (Molar mass = $142.04$ g/mol)

step_by_step.explanation: $n = M \times V$; mass = $n \times MM$
step_by_step.calculation: $n = 0.200 \text{ mol/L} \times 0.1000 \text{ L} = 0.0200 \text{ mol}$; mass = $0.0200 \text{ mol} \times 142.04 \text{ g/mol} = 2.841$ g

step_by_step.explanation: Weigh $2.841$ g on analytical balance; dissolve in ~$50$ mL water; transfer to $100.0$ mL volumetric flask; rinse beaker; dilute to mark; mix

worked_examples.final_answer: Weigh $2.841$ g $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$, dissolve, transfer to $100.0$ mL volumetric flask, dilute to mark, mix
worked_examples.concept_applied: Standard procedure for preparing solution from solid solute.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 2
worked_examples.problem: What volume of $6.00$ M $\text{HNO}_3$ is needed to prepare $250.0$ mL of $0.500$ M $\text{HNO}_3$? Describe the safe preparation procedure.

step_by_step.explanation: Wear PPE; add ~$150$ mL water to $250$ mL volumetric flask; slowly add $20.8$ mL conc. $\text{HNO}_3$ with swirling; cool; dilute to mark; mix

worked_examples.final_answer: Volume: $20.8$ mL; Procedure: add acid to water in volumetric flask, dilute to mark
worked_examples.concept_applied: Dilution calculation + safety protocol for concentrated acid.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 3
worked_examples.problem: A student prepares $0.100$ M NaOH by dissolving $2.00$ g NaOH in $500$ mL water. Why is this solution not exactly $0.100$ M, and how should its concentration be determined?

step_by_step.explanation: NaOH is hygroscopic (absorbs water) and reacts with $\text{CO}_2$; mass weighed includes impurities; volume measured with graduated cylinder (not volumetric flask)
Standardize by titration against primary standard KHP
step_by_step.calculation: Titrate known mass KHP with NaOH; calculate exact $M_{\text{NaOH}} = \text{mol KHP}/V_{\text{NaOH}}$

worked_examples.final_answer: NaOH absorbs water/$\text{CO}_2$; volume measurement approximate; standardize by titration against KHP
worked_examples.concept_applied: Unstable solutes require standardization; approximate preparation is acceptable if verified.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 4
worked_examples.problem: You have a $1.00$ M $\text{CuSO}_4$ stock solution. Describe how to prepare $100.0$ mL of $0.0100$ M $\text{CuSO}_4$ using serial dilutions.

step_by_step.explanation: $V_1 = (0.100 \text{ M} \times 100.0 \text{ mL})/1.00 \text{ M} = 10.0 \text{ mL}$; pipet $10.0$ mL stock into $100.0$ mL volumetric flask; dilute to mark; mix
Pipet $10.0$ mL of $0.100$ M solution into clean $100.0$ mL volumetric flask; dilute to mark; mix

worked_examples.final_answer: Two serial $1:10$ dilutions: $10.0$ mL stock → $100$ mL ($0.100$ M); then $10.0$ mL of that → $100$ mL ($0.0100$ M)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Serial dilutions prepare very dilute solutions accurately from concentrated stock.

worked_examples.title: Practice Problem 5
worked_examples.problem: A student reports preparing $0.100$ M HCl by adding $8.3$ mL of $12$ M HCl to $91.7$ mL water. Identify two errors in this procedure.

step_by_step.explanation: Volumes are not additive; $8.3$ mL acid + $91.7$ mL water ≠ exactly $100$ mL final volume
step_by_step.calculation: Correct: dilute to $100$ mL mark in volumetric flask

step_by_step.explanation: Adding acid to measured water is safer than adding water to acid; procedure should specify adding acid to water
step_by_step.calculation: Always add concentrated acid to water, not vice versa

step_by_step.explanation: Using graduated cylinder for $8.3$ mL lacks precision for $0.100$ M standard; volumetric pipet preferred
step_by_step.calculation: Match equipment precision to required accuracy

worked_examples.final_answer: Errors: (1) assumed volumes additive instead of diluting to mark; (2) safety: should add acid to water; (bonus) imprecise volume measurement
worked_examples.concept_applied: Proper technique requires diluting to mark, adding acid to water, and using precise equipment.

summary: Preparing solutions in the laboratory requires careful equipment selection, precise technique, and systematic procedures. Key principles: (1) Match equipment precision to required accuracy—analytical balance and volumetric glassware for standards; top-loading balance and graduated cylinders for approximate work. (2) For solid solutes: calculate mass, weigh accurately, dissolve completely, transfer quantitatively to volumetric flask, dilute to mark, mix thoroughly. (3) For dilutions: calculate stock volume using $C_1V_1 = C_2V_2$; always add concentrated reagents to water (not vice versa) for safety; dilute to final volume mark, not by adding calculated solvent volume. (4) Hydrated solutes require using hydrated molar mass for weighing; water of hydration becomes part of solvent. (5) Unstable solutes (e.g., NaOH) require standardization against a primary standard to determine exact concentration. Quality control steps include visual inspection, concentration verification (titration, spectrophotometry), and proper labeling. Common errors include: assuming volumes are additive, incomplete solute transfer, incorrect meniscus reading, temperature effects, and contamination. A systematic 6-check verification approach ensures accuracy: verify calculations, select appropriate equipment, execute technique correctly, inspect solution visually, label properly, and verify concentration if critical. Mastery of solution preparation techniques is essential for reliable experimental results in chemistry, biology, environmental science, and industrial applications. These skills bridge theoretical stoichiometry with practical laboratory work, ensuring that calculated concentrations are achieved in practice.
